summary: Gruber notes that three respected developers independently tried switching from iPhone to Android and all found it significantly lacking. Rather than celebrating, he expresses disappointment, arguing that strong competition is essential for driving Apple's innovation. He contends that Android's best opportunity lies not in chasing mass market share but in targeting the high end — building a single, premium phone per year that beats the iPhone in specific areas. He advises Android makers to copy Apple's successful strategies (annual release cadence, excellent build quality, clean branding) while exploiting Apple's weaknesses (App Store restrictions, MobileMe costs, battery life). The piece echoes his earlier prediction that Android would struggle unless someone took full control of both hardware and software quality.
key_points: Three well-regarded developers tried Android and all found it significantly behind the iPhone, suggesting the gap is widening rather than closing
Competition benefits everyone including Apple users — a monoculture drives complacency, not innovation
Android manufacturers should target the high end rather than the middle of the market, aiming to be Porsche to Apple's BMW
Google's reliance on hardware partners puts Android's final product in the hands of companies historically incompetent at design and hostile to users
The App Store controversy has opened the minds of developers and tech enthusiasts to switching, creating a real opportunity for a premium Android phone
money_quotes: A monoculture benefits no one in the long run, because it's competition that drives innovation.

Google's dependence on hardware and carrier partners puts the final product out of their control — and into the control of companies whose histories have shown them to be incompetent at design and hostile to users.
They don't want to downgrade from the iPhone. They want to upgrade from the iPhone.
Don't aim for the middle of the market. That seems to be what all the other Android manufacturers are doing and it's the road to NobodyCaresAboutYourPhoneVille.
If Apple is BMW, you can be Porsche.
The press is itching to write 'iPhone No Longer King of the Hill' headlines.
Web browsing needs to be iPhone quality, not 'almost iPhone quality'.
If your goal is to sell more smartphones than Apple, you're going to fail. If your hope is to gain a strong foothold in the market with a sub-par device, you are mistaken.
key_insight: The real Android opportunity isn't competing with the iPhone on volume — it's building a single, uncompromising premium device that gives disenchanted power users a reason to upgrade rather than downgrade.
